emails.subject: 🧰 The Builders' Issue: Codex, ElevenReader, Composer 1.5, and $125K in Funded AI Development
emails.body:
View this post on the web at https://aicollective.substack.com/p/the-builders-issue-codex-elevenreader

Itâs Wednesday, February 11th: This week, we spotlight OpenAIâs parallel agent command center Codex, ElevenLabsâ end-to-end audiobook production suite, and Cursorâs reasoning-heavy Composer 1.5âplus a $125,000 funded AI development opportunity and high-leverage roles for builders.
Head over to our Events Portal [ https://substack.com/redirect/327a11a8-32f0-4622-9cb0-686fb2e66008?j=eyJ1IjoiM3J0OW5nIn0.sEw6r2ua7eqZyk0Tqslbdm-LriK_wFQad1xrjGr2Pek ] to get the latest on upcoming AI Collective events near you. Search by city, date, or event format, and join thousands of builders at events across 100+ chapters on every continent (except Antarctica, for now). 
ð Based in SF? Check out SF IRL [ https://substack.com/redirect/c23f1e66-f64f-4325-a0f8-e381f97be1ec?j=eyJ1IjoiM3J0OW5nIn0.sEw6r2ua7eqZyk0Tqslbdm-LriK_wFQad1xrjGr2Pek ], MLOps SF [ https://substack.com/redirect/3c741269-3c40-4429-b8f6-87014de993e7?j=eyJ1IjoiM3J0OW5nIn0.sEw6r2ua7eqZyk0Tqslbdm-LriK_wFQad1xrjGr2Pek ], GenerativeAISF [ https://substack.com/redirect/3c25de37-fb0b-4552-933f-8230fb7345ed?j=eyJ1IjoiM3J0OW5nIn0.sEw6r2ua7eqZyk0Tqslbdm-LriK_wFQad1xrjGr2Pek ], or Cerebral Valleyâs spreadsheet [ https://substack.com/redirect/96569648-ea1b-49cf-8895-6f9f87557ba6?j=eyJ1IjoiM3J0OW5nIn0.sEw6r2ua7eqZyk0Tqslbdm-LriK_wFQad1xrjGr2Pek ] for more!
In Todayâs Top Tools, we spotlight some of the most innovative, creative AI apps that we recommend adding to your stack. 
ð» Codex App
OpenAIâs new macOS desktop app is a command center for orchestrating multiple AI coding agents in parallel. Run agents in separate threads organized by projects, switch between tasks without losing context, and manage long-running workflows with built-in worktrees and Git integration. Powered by GPT-5.3-Codex â the most capable agentic coding model to date â which combines frontier coding performance with 25% faster inference. The first model that was instrumental in creating itself: OpenAIâs team used early versions to debug its own training and deployment.
ð§ Audiobooks by ElevenLabs
A complete production suite that takes authors from manuscript to published audiobook in a single workflow. Upload your ePub, PDF, or docx, choose from 10,000+ expressive AI voices (or clone your own), edit pronunciations, and distribute directly to Spotify, Rakuten Kobo, Barnes & Noble, and ElevenReader. Automatic character detection assigns unique voices to each character in fiction. Most authors go from manuscript to finished audiobook in days vs. the traditional 3-6 months. ElevenReader offers 60% royalty per sale with no exclusivity requirements.
ð Composer 1.5
Cursorâs latest in-house agentic coding model trained with 20x more reinforcement learning steps than its predecessor. Composer 1.5 uses âthinking tokensâ to plan and reason through complex problems before generating code, plus a self-summarization mechanism that lets it maintain context across massive codebases. The model adapts its behavior based on task complexity â cracking simple questions fast while investing significantly more reasoning time on hard problems. Strikes the balance between intelligence and speed that makes it viable as a daily driver.
Join 150K+ AI Collective members getting curated news, tools, and events every week. Subscribe to stay ahead.
Here are a few standout opportunities from companies building at the edge of AI. Each role is selected for impact, growth potential, and relevance to our community.
Founding Engineer, item, San Francisco ($140Kâ$200K + 0.75%â1.50%): [ https://substack.com/redirect/a4df4b02-b5c9-4661-9384-b6c4e14ddeeb?j=eyJ1IjoiM3J0OW5nIn0.sEw6r2ua7eqZyk0Tqslbdm-LriK_wFQad1xrjGr2Pek ] âWeâre hiring a Founding Engineer to invent new patterns for how humans and agents work together. Youâll architect systems for the first agentic CRMâalready running 100K+ tasks per monthâand join a team of AI-forward builders who ship with Cursor and Claude Code.â
Machine Learning Intern, Human Archive, SF / Remote US ($6Kâ$10K/month): [ https://substack.com/redirect/54a36081-797a-46ad-b5d1-abf4ff9a7dbc?j=eyJ1IjoiM3J0OW5nIn0.sEw6r2ua7eqZyk0Tqslbdm-LriK_wFQad1xrjGr2Pek ] âJoin a robotics data lab founded by Stanford and Berkeley dropouts as a Machine Learning Intern. Youâll build ML infrastructure for next-gen robots, including VLA model benchmarking and multimodal alignment tooling, on a direct path to a founding engineer role.â
Founding Designer, telli, Berlin (â¬75Kââ¬160K): [ https://substack.com/redirect/b5f14a82-a0cb-4eca-85e3-0bf81f4d235a?j=eyJ1IjoiM3J0OW5nIn0.sEw6r2ua7eqZyk0Tqslbdm-LriK_wFQad1xrjGr2Pek ] âWe are looking for a Founding Designer to create intuitive interfaces for the first AI-native phone system. With >$2M ARR already, you will be the first full-time designer owning UX/UI across voice agent workflows where traditional UI patterns no longer apply.â
Chief of Staff to CEO, Nucleo, San Francisco / Remote ($60Kâ$120K + 0.50%â1.50%): [ https://substack.com/redirect/edd8f3bb-cd66-434c-b50b-efb1430929d9?j=eyJ1IjoiM3J0OW5nIn0.sEw6r2ua7eqZyk0Tqslbdm-LriK_wFQad1xrjGr2Pek ] âHelp us reduce cancer treatment wait times from ~10 weeks to ~10 days as Chief of Staff. Youâll support the first agentic platform for oncology CT scans, working alongside partners like Stanford and Cedars-Sinai to automate critical clinical workflows.â
Social Media Content Creator, Brickwise, London ($400â$1K/month): [ https://substack.com/redirect/25329a77-ac07-41d2-b20c-06bc7444130e?j=eyJ1IjoiM3J0OW5nIn0.sEw6r2ua7eqZyk0Tqslbdm-LriK_wFQad1xrjGr2Pek ] âBe the face and voice of an AI property manager as our Social Media Content Creator. Youâll drive growth through daily live sessions and short-form clips, showcasing how our AI handles tenant communications 24/7 across WhatsApp, SMS, and email.â
ð Community Notes
ð½ Limited Time Discount! U.S. Immigration Solved for People Teams
People teams are being asked to do the impossible: hire globally, stay compliant, and move fast, all while immigration policies shift under their feet. Ellis makes U.S. immigration finally feel manageable by combining expert-led case strategy with modern tooling, so People Operations can support employees through visa applications without drowning in spreadsheets or email threads.
Whether youâre sponsoring your first H-1B or scaling a full mobility program, Ellis gives you a clear system: visibility into every case, proactive timelines, and a partner who actually feels like part of your team. Immigration doesnât have to be chaotic and it definitely shouldnât be a black box.
With the H-1B lottery registration just around the corner Ellis has a limited-time discount for readers of The AI Collective!
ðï¸ Submit Your Project, Get Access to Free Agentic AI Development
People teams and engineering leaders are often tasked with driving productivity gains without the massive upfront R&D budget. Avahi, a Premier Tier AWS Partner, makes leveraging AI finally feel manageable by helping hundreds of companies implement real-world agentic solutions.
Whether youâre automating internal workflows or building customer-facing agents, Avahi provides the expert strategy and engineering muscle to move from concept to production. 
Weâre excited to offer readers of The AI Collective a limited-time opportunity: get your agentic AI development fully funded by AWSâa value of up to $125,000.
Our Premier Partner: Roam [ https://substack.com/redirect/fa542b6e-266a-4178-ba48-8cab3a01fde5?j=eyJ1IjoiM3J0OW5nIn0.sEw6r2ua7eqZyk0Tqslbdm-LriK_wFQad1xrjGr2Pek ]
Roam is the virtual workspace our team relies on to stay connected across time zones. It makes collaboration feel natural with shared spaces, private rooms, and built-in AI tools.
Roamâs focus on human-centered collaboration is why theyâre our Premier Partner, supporting our mission to connect the builders and leaders shaping the future of AI.
Experience Roam yourself with a free 14-day trial!
â¡ï¸ Before You Go
Partner With Us
Launching a new product or hosting an event? Put your work in front of our global audience of builders, founders, and operators â we feature select products and announcements that offer real value to our readers.
ð To be featured or sponsor a placement, reach out to our team.
The AI Collective is a community of volunteers, made for volunteers. All proceeds directly fund future initiatives that benefit this community.
Stay Connected

emails.subject: [Golden Talent Program] Waterloo Builders + LinkedIn Talent Velocity Report
emails.body:
Hello, hello!

I wanted to share a few updates that may be helpful.

First, the *Waterloo Venture Club *shared a list
<https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1TE-XMWba2mRbUaq9P5W5wsfkZfFbxq4FOHuvxXAHUQQ/edit?gid=1497023162#gid=1497023162>
of top builders from Waterloo with me. The list includes portfolio links
along with additional context on each student. Iâve worked to try and organize
the notes by separating fourth-years (who are likely exploring full-time
opportunities) from non-fourth-years (who are likely seeking internships).

I hope this makes it easier to quickly identify strong candidates aligned
with your current hiring needs. Please let me know if it would be helpful
to make introductions or provide any additional context.

Additionally, I came across a deck that I found particularly
interesting: *LinkedInâs
2026 Talent Velocity Advantage Report
<https://learning.linkedin.com/content/dam/me/business/en-us/amp/learning-solutions/images/lls-linkedin-talent-report-2026/pdfs/2026-linkedin-talent-velocity-advantage-report.pdf>.*
The report explores emerging talent trends, evolving skill demands, and how
companies can build competitive advantage through hiring and internal
mobility strategies. I thought it offered some helpful perspective on where
talent markets are heading and how early engagement with high-potential
builders can create outsized returns.

As always, feel free to reach out to me directly if you would like me to
feature an open role in Jobs of the Week or if you need support on any open
roles.

Warm regards,

--
*Alison Kaizer*
Partner, Talent
*Golden Ventures* <https://www.golden.ventures/> | *Work at a Portfolio
Company* <https://jobs.golden.ventures/jobs> | *Frequently Asked Questions*
<https://faq.golden.ventures/>

*Golden Ventures is a Founding Partner & Co-Organizer of Toronto Tech Week,
returning May 25â29, 2026 ð. Read the announcement
<https://betakit.com/toronto-tech-week-returns-in-2026/> and subscribe to
the TTW newsletter <https://www.torontotechweek.com/> to stay up to date.*

emails.body:
Hello, hello!

*Please note all contents of this email are strictly confidential. This
email is NOT to be forwarded outside of your organization. Please respect
candidate confidentiality.*

This week we've connected with a number of new candidates. Weâve summarized
their profiles for you below. If youâd like an introduction, please click
on *REQUEST INTRODUCTION.* If the candidate is interested as well, we will
connect you directly.

Note: Occasionally, we come across high-quality candidates who would like
to keep their search *completely private*. These candidates are not
featured in the list below. To gain access to our private candidates,
please complete the *Key Hire Form*
<https://airtable.com/appvDjEn1ucqoddnu/pagpEeLR5sYRmCUcz/form>. If one of

emails.subject: Musk's moonshot
emails.body:
**Welcome back.**

Half of xAI's founding team has now walked out the door, but Elon Musk isn't sweating the talent exodusâhe's too busy pitching employees on moon factories and AI satellite catapults (?) ahead of what could be the largest IPO in history.

Letâs dive in.

_ _ 

----------

Caption: 

[â](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/02/09/technology/social-media-addiction-trial.html)** Meta and YouTube Face âDigital Casinoâ Reckoning in Court** 
In a Los Angeles courtroom, lawyers for a 20-year-old plaintiff argued that Instagram and YouTube were engineered like âdigital casinos,â using infinite scroll, autoplay and algorithmic nudges to hook childrenâwhile Meta countered that her mental-health struggles stemmed from family trauma, not product designâmarking the first major jury test of whether social media platforms can be held liable for addiction in the same way as tobacco or gambling firms
 
**[â](https://www.theinformation.com/briefings/departures-accelerate-elon-musks-xai-yet-another-cofounder-leaves?rc=ghbbgh)**** Cofounder Exodus at xAI After SpaceX Merger** 
Jimmy Ba, a cofounder of Elon Muskâs xAI and a respected AI researcher, has departed alongside other senior staff just days after SpaceX merged with the company at eye-watering valuations, raising fresh questions about internal stability as Musk fuses rockets, satellites and frontier models into a single capital-intensive empire
 
**[â](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/02/11/mistral-ai-infrastructure-sweden.html)**** Mistral Commits â¬1.2 Billion to Swedish AI Infrastructure**
Franceâs Mistral is investing â¬1.2 billion in AI data centers and compute capacity in Swedenâits first major infrastructure push outside Franceâas Europe races to secure technological sovereignty and build a homegrown AI cloud capable of rivaling American hyperscalers 


----------View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/4fdf63c9-ad3a-4960-b2e3-2cb30f5c14aa/strategy_blue_anim_one_loop.gif?t=1753437621)
Caption: 

###### News Analysis | **To the moon?**

### _**Musk pitches moonshot while xAIâs founding team collapses**_

----------
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/bad324ca-a5fa-420b-ae0e-7756e7f4b7be/moon.jpg?t=1770817074)
Follow image link: (https://thatstartupguy.beehiiv.com/upgrade)
Caption: 


--------------------
**Whatâs Happening**
Half of xAI's twelve-person founding team has now left the company, with co-founders Tony Wu and Jimmy Ba announcing their departures within hours of each other this week. Rather than address the leadership exodus directly, Musk convened an all-hands meeting Tuesday evening to tell employees that â¦


--------------------

Caption:

Want full access to our best stuff?

_**What youâre missing:**_

* _The executive exodus that should worry IPO investors_

* _Why Musk's moon pivot blindsided his own team_

* _The space law loophole underwriting a $1.25T bet_


--------------------

---------- 

----------

Caption: 

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/db71dd59-29e8-4049-b868-bef0b8af887d/accelerate.jpg?t=1770816596)
Follow image link: (https://www.wired.com/story/ai-industry-rivals-are-teaming-up-on-a-startup-accelerator/)
Caption: 

**[â](https://www.wired.com/story/ai-industry-rivals-are-teaming-up-on-a-startup-accelerator/)** **AI Industry Rivals Are Teaming Up on a Startup Accelerator**
OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, Microsoft and even Mistral have declared a brief dÃ©tente to back F/ai, a Paris-based accelerator at Station F that showers European startups with $1M+ in model and compute creditsâless a peace treaty than a land grab to lock founders into their ecosystems before loyalties harden and switching costs bite
 
**[â](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/02/10/technology/personaltech/ai-google-meta-opt-out.html)** **A.I. Is Giving You a Personalized Internet, but You Have No Say in It**
Google and Meta are quietly baking AI assistants into Gmail, Search, Instagram and WhatsApp with no meaningful opt-out, steering users toward a hyper-personalized web where ads, prices and even information vary by promptâconvenient on the surface, but ultimately a bid to rewire digital advertising around chatbot-driven data exhaust
 
**[â](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/02/08/style/ai-tech-san-francisco.html)** **These A.I. Dreamers Donât Fit the Stereotype**
In San Francisco, 20-something founders with modest upbringings and minimal coding rÃ©sumÃ©s are chasing AI fortunes from cramped apartments and coffee shops, wagering that this capital-soaked âcowboy eraâ will mint generational wealthâeven as they concede, with admirable candor, that most rockets explode on the launchpad.till beat flashy hardwareâat least until local rivals fix their launch timing


----------View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/4fdf63c9-ad3a-4960-b2e3-2cb30f5c14aa/strategy_blue_anim_one_loop.gif?t=1753437621)
Caption: 

###### News Analysis | **Cold compute**

### _**Mistral plants its flag in Sweden**_

----------
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/ba8f5595-687a-4557-b21d-be54e9f41fe9/mistral.jpg?t=1770816493)
Follow image link: (https://thatstartupguy.beehiiv.com/upgrade)
Caption: 




--------------------
**Whatâs Happening**
Europe's most valuable AI startup is going vertical. Mistral AI announced a â¬1.2 billion ($1.4 billion) investment to build AI data centers in Sweden â its first infrastructure play outside France â in partnership with local provider EcoDataCenter. 

The facility, set to open in 2027 in BorlÃ¤nge and powered by NVIDIA's next-gen Vera Rubin GPUs, is designed to train and run Mistral's models on European soil with European energy. The move comes alongside a staggering revenue disclosure: CEO Arthur Mensch told the Financial Times that â¦


--------------------
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/ba05790b-b8f9-4f69-a529-98b84cf7c990/Subscribers_only_with_lock__transparent_.png?t=1765488456)

Want full access to our best stuff?

_**What youâre missing:**_

* _The infrastructure strategy that lets Mistral double-dip on its GPUs_

* _Why Sweden's geography is Europe's secret AI weapon_

* _The revenue gap Mistral still has to close_


---------- 

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/6f23f859-2a04-469c-a5dd-5e74e43df7b7/VC_Corner_pyramids.png?t=1743020798)
Caption: 

----------
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/5d7340de-b167-4480-8538-361dd6506b0b/github.jpg?t=1770815996)
Follow image link: (https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-10/former-github-ceo-thomas-dohmke-raises-60-million-for-new-startup?srnd=phx-technology)
Caption: 




--------------------
#### **â**** ****Deal of the Day**

Former GitHub CEO Thomas Dohmke raised $60 million at a $300 million valuation for Entire, a developer tools startup building context and provenance tracking for AI-generated code. 

The size of the check reflects a simple reality: AI-authored software is scaling faster than any organization's ability to govern it, and the tooling layer that tracks agent behavior, intent, and provenance barely exists. 

Dohmke is not building another coding assistant but positioning Entire as the system of record for AI-authored software, the audit and context layer every enterprise will eventually require before shipping agent-generated code to production. 

A $300 million seed for an open source project with no revenue is Felicis pricing the founder, not the product, betting the former GitHub CEO can establish default status in a category that did not exist eighteen months ago. 

Microsoft's venture arm co-investing signals less conviction in the startup than a hedge: if the context layer emerges outside GitHub, Redmond wants a seat.

**What to Watch Next**
Whether GitHub, Cursor, or Anthropic ship native context-tracking features in the next two quarters, which would reframe Entire's open source strategy from distribution advantage to defensive necessity

----------

**[â](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-10/nebius-agrees-to-buy-ai-agent-search-company-tavily-for-275-million?srnd=phx-technology)**** Nebius to Buy Tavily for $275M** â The ex-Yandex cloud player is snapping up an âAI agent searchâ layer so its bots can browse the web without face-planting into broken links and stale pages
 
**[â](https://www.ft.com/content/cba54e86-2b2a-422c-861b-dc9280d6aa65)** **Olix Raises $220M** â A secretive UK chip startup is betting that inference-hungry AI agents will need new accelerator designs, not just ever-larger piles of Nvidia GPUs
 
**[â](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-10/blackstone-joins-anthropic-round-raising-stake-to-1-billion?srnd=phx-technology)** **Blackstone joins** **Anthropic Round** â Blackstone is leaning further into the Claude maker, lifting its total stake to roughly $1B and underwriting the idea that âAI picks-and-shovelsâ are now an asset class
 
**[â](https://techcrunch.com/2026/02/10/vega-raises-120m-series-b-to-rethink-how-enterprises-detect-cyber-threats/)** **Vega Raises $120M Series B** â The startup wants to rebuild the security operations center around AI-native detection, treating alerts like noisy data to be triaged automatically instead of lovingly escalated by humans at 3 a.m.
 
**[â](https://techcrunch.com/2026/02/10/primary-ventures-raises-massive-625m-fund-v-to-focus-on-seed-investing/)** **Primary Ventures Raises $625M Fund V** â The seed firmâs new fund is a quiet tell that âearly-stageâ has inflated in the AI era, with check sizes and support expectations rising right along with compute bills


----------âââââââââââââââââââââââââââ

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/ba45286f-4769-46ee-9006-0d5973d48a3d/one_more._thing.png?t=1764072872)
Caption:

âââââââââââââââââââââââââââ

Thatâs all for today!

emails.subject: Shipping Fast And Iterating At AI-speed | Takeaways For Founders And Product Leaders
emails.body:
View this post on the web at https://labs.adaline.ai/p/shipping-fast-and-iterating-at-ai

TLDR: Shipping Fast and Iterating at AI Speed" explores why traditional startup speed advice fails in AI development. The blog argues that real AI speed isn't about moving faster than competitors, but about learning velocityâunderstanding what "good" looks like and adapting quickly. It covers how short-term velocity destroys long-term progress through technical debt, why correctness is subjective in AI products, and how sustainable speed requires informed restraint, clear ownership, and reversible decisions. Readers will learn concrete principles from industry leaders on building feedback loops, maintaining team confidence through transparency, and designing systems flexible enough to survive the AI ecosystem's rapid changes. The key insight: the fastest teams avoid premature bets and focus on preserving optionality while maintaining strong signals about what matters.
Introduction
Founder Intro: Shipping Fast and Iterating at AI Speed
âMove fastâ has always been a startup mantra. In AI, that advice has become dangerously ambiguous.
Teams ship more often than ever. Demos come together in days. Iteration feels constant. And yet, many companies still feel stuck, slowed not by lack of activity but by a lack of clarity.
Panel 4 was designed to unpack that tension. Rather than asking, âHow do we ship faster?â, we wanted to ask a more precise question: What does speed actually mean when youâre building AI products, and what quietly destroys it over time?
To explore that, we brought together operators who are shipping at the edge of whatâs possible, across very different contexts:
Daksh Gupta [ https://substack.com/redirect/737fe552-e5d8-481e-86c1-49664acb49c3?j=eyJ1IjoiNWc4N2VvIn0.i1yBnIQu-yyG5WO7bOLXUfqLasCV2M4mch-39k2GODs ], Co-founder and CEO at Greptile, is building AI systems where correctness and iteration speed must coexist.
Evan Owen [ https://substack.com/redirect/af37651d-364b-48bb-a41d-877edfd4ecde?j=eyJ1IjoiNWc4N2VvIn0.i1yBnIQu-yyG5WO7bOLXUfqLasCV2M4mch-39k2GODs ], Co-founder and CEO at Glue, is navigating fast-moving AI workflows where trust and learning loops matter more than raw throughput.
Ray Jang [ https://substack.com/redirect/03ef0b1e-e8a6-4b91-a506-5ac1a3759992?j=eyJ1IjoiNWc4N2VvIn0.i1yBnIQu-yyG5WO7bOLXUfqLasCV2M4mch-39k2GODs ], [ https://substack.com/redirect/03ef0b1e-e8a6-4b91-a506-5ac1a3759992?j=eyJ1IjoiNWc4N2VvIn0.i1yBnIQu-yyG5WO7bOLXUfqLasCV2M4mch-39k2GODs ] Co-founder and CEO at Atria, operates at the intersection of automation, experimentation, and reliability.
Yen Tan [ https://substack.com/redirect/83286a91-4aa6-4de2-9d3a-8483a6e2bad6?j=eyJ1IjoiNWc4N2VvIn0.i1yBnIQu-yyG5WO7bOLXUfqLasCV2M4mch-39k2GODs ], Product Manager at 15Five, is bringing a product and user-centered lens to shipping in high-trust environments.
What emerged was a clear reframing of AI speed. This panel wasnât about shipping more features or chasing every new model release. It was about learning velocity: how quickly teams understand what good looks like, detect when something feels off, and correct course without eroding trust.
Across the conversation, a consistent theme surfaced:
âSpeed without direction is just noise.â
âSustainable speed comes from tight feedback loops, informed restraint, and organizations designed to learn.â
The sections that follow break down what that looks like in practice, from why dogfooding beats dashboards early, to how feature flags enable safe aggression, to why trust behaves like a finite resource.
If youâre building with AI and feel like youâre moving fast but not forward, this panel offers a grounded perspective on what real velocity actually requires.
1. AI Speed Is About Learning What âGoodâ Looks Like
The panel opened by dismantling a common misconception: AI speed does not simply mean shipping faster.
Shipping faster is easy. Learning faster is hard.
What separates teams that actually move quickly from those that just move often is how fast they develop a shared understanding of quality.
Speed Comes From Signal, Not Velocity
Across the discussion, speakers converged on a more precise definition of AI speed.
AI speed is defined by:
How quickly teams learn what âgoodâ outputs look like?
How fastÂ can they tell when something feelsÂ off?
How earlyÂ can they course-correct withoutÂ breaking trust?
As Daksh Gupta, Co-founder and CEO at Greptile, emphasized, most AI teams donât slow down because they ship too little. They slow down because they donât know what to aim for.
Without a clear target, iteration becomes noise.
Correctness Is Often Ambiguous in AI Products
In traditional software, correctness is binary. Something works, or it doesnât.
In AI products, correctness is often subjective.
As Yen Tan, Product Manager at 15Five, described, this ambiguity shows up most clearly in:
Creative workflows,
Generative systems,
Judgment-based tasks,
Assistive experiences.
Outputs can be plausible without being good. They can be technically correct, but emotionally wrong. They can pass automated checks, and still fail user expectations.
This makes iteration fundamentally harder.
Without Quality Signals, Teams Thrash
Several speakers described a familiar failure mode:
Teams ship quickly,
Outputs look reasonable,
Feedback is vague,
Iteration continues blindly.
As Ray Jang, Co-founder and CEO at Atria, noted, without fast, reliable signals on quality, teams end up oscillatingâchanging prompts, models, or workflows without knowing whether theyâre actually improving anything.
The result is activity without progress.
âFeels Offâ Is an Important Signal
One of the more subtle insights from the panel was the importance of intuition early on.
As Evan Owen, Co-founder and CEO at Glue, explained, experienced teams learn to trust early discomfort. When outputs feel offâeven if they technically passâthatâs often the first indicator that assumptions are wrong, or constraints are missing.
Teams that move fast donât ignore that signal. They investigate it immediately.
Speed comes from shortening the gap between:
Noticing something feels wrong,
Understanding why,
Fixing the underlying cause.
Directional Clarity Beats Raw Throughput
The panel repeatedly returned to the idea that speed without direction is wasted motion.
AI makes it easy to:
Generate more outputs,
Try more variations,
Explore more options.
But without a shared definition of âgood,â those options donât converge.
As one speaker summarized:
The fastest teams arenât the ones shipping the most changes,
Theyâre the ones learning what to keep.
The Practical Takeaway
AI speed isnât about how fast you deploy. Itâs about how fast you learn.
Teams that truly move quickly:
Define quality early,
Develop strong instincts for âwrongâ,
Create tight feedback loops,
Correct course before problems compound.
In AI products, learning velocity beats shipping velocity.
Speed without clarity feels productiveâuntil it isnât.
2. Short-Term Velocity Can Destroy Long-Term Velocity
One of the most consistent warnings across the panel was a counterintuitive one:
The fastest way to slow down permanently is to optimize too aggressively for short-term speed.
In an ecosystem that rewards quick demos and rapid iteration, this is an easy trap to fall into, and a hard one to escape.
Early Momentum Often Comes From Fragile Choices
Several speakers described how teams often gain early momentum by making expedient decisions:
Choosing frameworks optimized for speed over control.
Hardcoding integrations instead of designing interfaces.
Building around temporary standards.
Overfitting workflows to current model capabilities.
These choices feel rational in the moment. They produce visible progress. They reduce upfront friction.
As Daksh Gupta, Co-founder & CEO of Greptile, explained, many of these decisions arenât mistakes. Theyâre unexamined commitments that accumulate quietly.
The Hidden Cost of Expedience
What looks like speed early often shows up later as a constraint.
As products mature, those early shortcuts create:
Architectural lock-in.
Brittle abstractions.
Painful migrations.
Slow, risky changes.
Fear of touching core systems.
As Ray Jang, Co-founder & CEO of Atria, noted, teams often donât realize theyâve slowed down until theyâre already stuck. Every change requires workarounds. Every improvement risks regression. Momentum evaporates.
The system becomes fast to run, but slow to change.
AI Ecosystems Shift Faster Than Architecture
This problem is amplified in AI because the ecosystem itself is moving so quickly:
Models evolve.
APIs change.
Best practices shift.
Capabilities that felt stable six months ago suddenly arenât.
As Evan Owen, Co-founder & CEO of Glue, pointed out, decisions that assume todayâs model behavior will persist are especially dangerous. Overfitting to current capabilities may unlock speed now, but it creates fragility later, precisely when adaptation matters most.
Overfitting Is a Form of Technical Debt
The panel reframed overfitting in a broader sense.
Itâs not just about data or prompts. Itâs about designing systems that only work under narrow conditions.
Overfit systems:
Assume specific output formats.
Rely on implicit model behavior.
Break when context windows change.
Fail when reasoning patterns shift.
Each assumption tightens the systemâs tolerance for change.
True Speed Requires Optionality
The teams that sustained velocity over time shared one trait: informed restraint.
They:
Moved fast where reversibility was high.
Slowed down where decisions were expensive to undo.
Avoided locking in assumptions prematurely.
Designed interfaces, not shortcuts.

The panel also reframed how teams should think about decision quality.
Perfect decisions are rare. Reversible decisions are common.
Fast AI teams:
Make decisions quickly.
Revisit them often.
Correct course early.
Avoid over-indexing on certainty.
As Evan Owen, Co-founder & CEO of Glue, put it, teams that wait for confidence rarely get it. Teams that act and observe learn faster. Speed comes from motion with feedback â not deliberation without data.
Learning Loops Are the Real Differentiator
Across all examples, one pattern dominated:
The fastest AI companies have the tightest learning loops.
They:
Ship small changes.
Observe real behavior.
Absorb feedback directly.
Adjust immediately.
Tooling supports this â but it doesnât create it.
Learning loops are designed through:
Org structure.
Incentives.
Ownership.
Trust.
The Final Reframe
By the end of the panel, âAI speedâ had been fully redefined.
It isnât about:
Better prompts.
Faster GPUs.
Clever architectures.
Itâs about:
Collapsing feedback loops.
Reducing organizational drag.
Empowering decision-makers.
Learning faster than competitors.
The Practical Takeaway
If your AI team feels slow, the bottleneck is rarely technical. Itâs usually:
Unclear ownership.
Delayed decisions.
Filtered feedback.
Cultural friction.
The fastest teams donât just build better systems. They build organizations designed to learn at AI speed. And in an ecosystem where technology converges quickly, learning speed is the only durable advantage left.
Thanks for reading Adaline Labs! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

emails.body:
Afternoon Moe, saw on my feed that you liked with a post about startup
funding trends.


Given Pica's core AI tooling platform for AI agents, curious how many of
your customers are also engaging with AI thought leaders?


1,100,000 people engage with Ai content every day. So we built a lead
generation engine *exclusively* for AI companies, which sifts through
millions of social interactions (like this one) to contact *only* the
prospects who are already interested in your offering.


Could I show you how we'd run it for Pica and get your feedback?


Best,

Colin Graham

Co-Founder & Principal Consultant

Thumbstop Media Ltd.



PS: If this isn't relevant, just let me know!

emails.subject: AI rewrites valuation math
emails.body:
Defense investors eye Japan regime change; US unicorns aging in place; ABS Global raises $100M for retail interval fund
February 11, 2026   |   Read online   |   Manage your subscription
The Daily Pitch
VC
Presented by SVB
 
ð Launching valuations estimates: There's a new tool to help assess the value of venture-backed companies between rounds. PitchBook Valuation Estimates use market data, company signals and pricing trends across peers to give dealmakers a real-time view into valuations. Speaking of valuations, the 2025 Annual US VC Valuations and Returns Report, sponsored by Morgan Stanley at Work, underscores AI's dominion.
 
AI Is rewriting VC valuationsâand putting old unicorns out to pasture
 
By Andrew Akers, Lead Quantitative Research Analyst

Several years into a venture capital correction, the typical startup valuation is higher than it was during the 2021 heyday.

Such is the power of AI, according to our latest US VC Valuations and Returns report. AI companies are commanding significant valuation premiums over non-AI companies and receiving valuation step-ups that are materially higher than those of other startups across all stages.

That momentum is most visible in the Bay Area, once again the center of gravity for venture capital. Fueled by a buildup of AI talent and capital, valuations of San Francisco-area companies have far outpaced those of the US market.

But the overexuberance of the last cycle is still tangible. The median IPO step-up in 2025 was, in fact, a step down, with more than half of newly public companies debuting at valuations below their most recent private roundsâa reminder that not every unicorn can outrun gravity.
Access more valuations data
 

Meanwhile, a large share of the US unicorn population is aging in place. Nearly half of the 857 US unicorns still carry valuations that are more than three years oldâa growing disconnect in a market that has otherwise moved on.

In our latest analyst note, we introduced PitchBook Valuation Estimates, a new analytical tool that gives timely, mark-to-market updates for VC-backed company valuations. An analysis of the US unicorn cohort with up-to-date valuation estimates revealed another example of bifurcation within the VC market.

In todayâs market, the number of US unicorns is likely closer to 600. Yet the estimated total value of this downsized group has soared in the past two years to $4.4 trillion, thanks to a handful of massive companies. The 10 largest unicorns account for more than 51% of the total value, the highest share in more than a decade.
Download the full analysis of US unicorns
 
 
A message from SVB  
SVB releases 2026 State of the Markets
 
SVBâs new report finds venture gaining steam â $340B invested and exits are up â while fundraising tightens and round timelines stretch.

Get the full report
 
Catch Up Quick  
â¢ ICapital, a marketplace for access to alternative funds, is acquiring a fund-administration fintech that will help the wealth-focused firm eliminate burdensome investor-onboarding paperwork and extend its reach to institutional clients. Read more

â¢ Banks are lining up to arrange financing for an InPost take-private. A consortium led by Advent International and FedEx is putting together a deal for the Polish parcelâlocker group. Go deeper

â¢ ABS Global Investments has raised about $100 million for its first interval fund and is now seeking to attract high-net-worth individuals, joining a growing number of firms turning to the wealth channel to attract new LPs. Read more
 
A hawkish Japan could mean opportunity for private capital
By Jim Corridore, Senior Industrials Research Analyst

Following Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's election win Sunday, Japanese stocks hit record highs on hopes that her policies will spur defense and technology investment.

This could also spell opportunity for the private markets.

For much of the post-war era, Japan's defense posture has been shaped by pacifism and strict limits on military spending. Under Article 9 of its constitution, the country confined its Self-Defense Forces to narrowly defensive roles, kept defense spending to around 1% of GDP and discouraged private capital from engaging with military technologies. The result was a closed aerospace and defense ecosystem dominated by large contractors, with limited room for VC or PE.

That model is now shifting.
 
In response to China's military expansion, North Korea's missile activity and a more fragile global security environment, Japan is moving toward a more assertive defense strategy. Budgets are rising with a target of 2% of GDP, capabilities are expanding beyond passive defense, and policymakers are increasingly supportive of dual-use innovation in areas like autonomy, space, sensors, cyber and advanced electronics.

This has begun to open the door to private capital in ways that would have been politically or structurally difficult a decade ago.

PitchBook data shows that VC and PE activity in Japan has been flat over the past five years. However, this may change as early-stage startups aligned with national security priorities attract funding. PE activity, however, has structural challenges: defense remains dominated by large companies, there are relatively few mid-market assets to acquire, carve-outs are rare, and procurement timelines and regulatory oversight complicate traditional buyout strategies.

For investors, the takeaway is that Japan is still early in its defense market transition. VC is capturing the initial wave through dual-use innovation, while PE opportunity is more likely to emerge later as companies scale, policies mature and the industrial base becomes more accessible to private capital.
Related research:
2025 Japan Private Capital Breakdown
Q4 2025 Defense Tech VC Trends
 
Side Letters  
Smart reads that caught our eye.

â¢ The US stock market is growing fatigued of AI companies, but investments in South Korea are trending the other way. Underneath the American SaaS-pocalypse, some investors think Korean companies are a hidden gem for diversifying assets. [Bloomberg]

â¢ Some Olympians' medals are falling apart. Organizers said an issue with the production of the medals by the Italian State Mint has been resolved, and repairs are being offered to affected athletes. [Reuters]

â¢ "I am institutionally trained not to get canceled." Meet Molly O'Shea, a podcaster deeply immersed in Silicon Valley culture with access to some of VC's biggest names. [Business Insider]

People  
Apollo Global Management hired Diego De Giorgi as incoming head of EMEA.

LBP AM appointed Ãmilie Goirand as head of ESG corporate debt and unlisted solutions.
 
VC Deals  
Payment infrastructure developer Stripe is considering launching a tender offer at a valuation over $140 billion, Axios reported.

Runway, a startup building AI video generation models, raised a $315 million Series E led by General Atlantic. The round values the company at $5.3 billion, TechCrunch reported.

Cloud computing startup Oxide Computer Company secured a $200 million Series C led by US Innovative Technology Fund.

Solace Health, which develops a healthcare advocacy platform, received a $130 million Series C led by IVP at a valuation over $1 billion.

Healthtech startup Garner Health raised an $118 million Series D led by Kleiner Perkins.

Biotech startup Iliad Biotechnologies secured a $115 million Series B led by RA Capital Management.

Bretton AI, the developer of AI used in financial crime investigations, raised a $75 million Series B led by Sapphire Ventures.

Paris-based corporate events procurement startup Naboo secured a $70 million Series B led by Lightspeed.

Entire, a startup building developer tools to manage AI-authored code, raised a $60 million seed round led by Felicis at a $300 million valuation.

Berlin-based energy metering startup Metiundo raised â¬40 million from Octopus Energy Generation.
 
Don't miss our upcoming webinar
 
In 2025, VC continued to grapple with fundraising and liquidity challenges despite a handful of high-profile exits. However, deal activity is beginning to accelerateâparticularly among first financingsâsparking renewed optimism about the market's growth in 2026.

In this webinar, experts from PitchBook, NVCA, J.P. Morgan, Dentons, and EisnerAmper will discuss findings from the Q4 2025 PitchBook-NVCA Venture Monitor, along with current VC trends, risks, and opportunities.

Register now to secure your spot.
 
PE Deals  
Abu Dhabi's Mubadala Capital agreed to acquire advertising group Clear Channel Outdoor in an all-cash deal valuing it at $6.2 billion, including debt, Bloomberg reported.

General Atlantic agreed to acquire Nasdaq-listed European Wax Center in a $330 million take-private deal.

The Carlyle Group agreed to acquire a majority stake in India-based home loan provider Nido Home Finance in a 21 billion rupee ($232 million) deal.

CapVest Partners entered exclusive discussions with HLD to acquire a majority stake in TSG, a French technical services provider for critical energy infrastructure.

Baird Capital acquired UK-based temperature-control hire solutions provider Rapid Energy.

KPS Capital Partners agreed to acquire structural concrete company The Wells Companies.

Harith General Partners is in advanced stages of acquiring South African budget carrier FlySafair.
 
Exits  
Inflexion Private Equity and other PE backers are considering a sale of UK-based Acorn Group, which provides home and auto insurance and could be valued around Â£1 billion in a deal, Bloomberg reported.

Lumina Capital Management-backed Brazilian fintech Agibank raised $240 million in its US IPO.

Dutch cloud computing company Nebius Group agreed to acquire Tavily, a New York-based AI agent search engine developer backed by Alpha Wave Global and Insight Partners.

New York-based financial data analysis startup Noetica, which is backed by Lightspeed, has been acquired by Thomson Reuters.

Fox purchased Canada-based podcast subscription platform developer Supercast, which is backed by investors including Syrus Partners and Horseplay Ventures.
 
Fundraising  
Primary Ventures closed on $625 million for its fifth fund, which is focused on early-stage investments in the US.
 
Investors  
MIO Partners, McKinsey & Company's investment arm with about $26 billion in AUM, agreed to join Neuberger Berman.
 
Chart of the Day
